1: A revelation of God: Paul names himself "an apostle of Christ Jesus by the will of God," placing Christ alongside the Father as the joint authority behind his commission. Christ is not merely the subject of Paul's message but the agent who called him — the same risen Lord who confronted him on the Damascus road. The letter's opening sentence announces that everything to follow flows from a divine initiative in Christ.
2: The Christological grammar of the salutation: grace and peace come "from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" as a single coordinated source. In ordinary Greek letters, greetings come from the sender alone. Paul's modification places Christ alongside the Father as a co-bestower of the defining gifts of the new covenant — grace and peace — which is an implicit claim about Christ's divine status.
3: A direct doxological statement: every spiritual blessing flows through Christ. The "heavenly realms" language anticipates the cosmic scope of Christ's exaltation (Ephesians 1:20-21) — the same sphere where believers are already seated with him (Ephesians 2:6). The priestly blessing of Numbers 6:24-26 blessed Israel with God's face and peace; what Christ has done is transfer that blessing into eschatological and personal reality for all who are in him.
4: A direct election-in-Christ claim: the choosing "in him before the foundation of the world" means Christ is not the mechanism of election but its sphere and ground — election has always been an election in the Elect One. The type from Deuteronomy 7:6 — Israel chosen as a holy people — finds its ultimate form here: the people of God are holy and blameless not by covenant obligation but by position in Christ, who is himself the Holy and Blameless One.
5: A type fulfilled: Israel's designation as God's "firstborn son" (Exodus 4:22) established adoption as the relational framework for the covenant people. Paul uses the same adoption language (huiothesia) but grounds it exclusively in Christ. Christ is the eternal Son; believers receive sonship by being placed "in him." The predestination to adoption is not a decree about individuals in isolation but about their destiny in the Son.
6: A revelation of God: the Son is called "the Beloved" (ho ēgapēmenos) — the same designation as the heavenly voice at Jesus's baptism ("This is my beloved Son," Matthew 3:17) and the transfiguration. Paul uses this title to ground his claim that grace has been "freely given us" in the One whom the Father loves with unique, eternal love. The grace that flows to believers is the overflow of the love the Father has for the Son.
7: A direct fulfillment: "redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of sins." The Passover and Levitical sacrificial system embedded in Israel's worship the logic that redemption costs life — blood makes atonement (Leviticus 17:11), and God redeemed Israel from Egypt with an outstretched arm (Exodus 6:6). Paul presents Christ's blood as the definitive event that fulfills and therefore terminates what those types kept pointing toward without accomplishing permanently.
8: The theme of divine wisdom lavished in Christ: the "wisdom and understanding" with which grace is dispensed echoes the Wisdom literature's description of God's creative intelligence (Proverbs 8:22-31; Wis Ephesians 7:22-24). The NT presents Christ as the one in whom "all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden" (Colossians 2:3). The abundance of grace in the cross is not a random act but the product of infinite wisdom working to a predetermined end.
9: A shadow now disclosed: the "mystery of his will" (mystērion tou thelēmatos autou) draws on Daniel's language about divine purposes hidden until God reveals them (Daniel 2:28-29). In Daniel, the mystery concerned the course of empires. The mystery Paul announces is larger: the entire content of God's hidden will is "purposed in Christ" — Christ is not one element of the plan; he is its content.
10: A direct Christological claim of cosmic scope: anakephalaiōsasthai — to sum up or recapitulate all things in Christ — presents Christ as the One in whom the fractured creation finds its unity restored. The Adamic disintegration of creation (Romans 8:20-22) moves toward its resolution when "all things in heaven and on earth" are brought under Christ's headship. This is not merely the church's story; it is the cosmos's story, with Christ as its conclusion.
11: The theme of divine sovereignty expressed through Christ: believers are "chosen" according to the plan "of him who works out everything in conformity with the purpose of his will." This language of purposeful divine working (the participle energountos) is the same language Paul uses for the power that raised Christ (Ephesians 1:19-20). The election that precedes human history is executed by the same power that vindicates Christ.
12: The theme of God's glory as the final cause of election: "that we... might be for the praise of his glory" defines the telos of being chosen in Christ. In Isaiah, Israel was created "for my glory" (Isaiah 43:7); Paul universalizes this to all who are "first to hope in Christ." The display of God's glory now runs through Christ and through those gathered to him — the community of the new exodus.
13: A direct fulfillment: the sealing of believers by the Holy Spirit actualizes Ezekiel's new-covenant promise — "I will put my Spirit within you" (Ezekiel 36:27). The Spirit is given specifically "when you believed" the gospel of salvation — faith in Christ is the entry point into the covenant community marked by divine indwelling. The promised Spirit is also the "Spirit of Christ" (Romans 8:9), given because Christ has inaugurated the new covenant.
14: The theme of inheritance reconceived in Christ: the Promised Land was Israel's inheritance — a concrete, geopolitical guarantee of God's covenant faithfulness. The Holy Spirit as "deposit guaranteeing our inheritance" signals that the inheritance is now eschatological and personal: full participation in the new creation that Christ's resurrection inaugurated. The down payment is real but the fullness belongs to "the day of redemption."
15: A revelation of God: even Paul's grateful recognition of the Ephesians' faith demonstrates that the marks of covenant belonging have shifted from Torah observance to "faith in the Lord Jesus and love for all the saints." Christ has become the organizing center of the covenant community — to belong is to be in him, and to be in him is to be bound to all who share that identity.
16: The theme of intercessory prayer shaped by Christ: Paul's unceasing thanksgiving and prayer for others mirrors the intercession of Christ, who "always lives to make intercession" for those who come to God through him (Hebrews 7:25). The prayer-life of the apostle is a participation in the ongoing priestly work of the ascended Christ.
17: The theme of the Messiah's Spirit now given to believers: the prayer for "the Spirit of wisdom and revelation" for knowing God draws on the Isaianic promise that the Spirit of wisdom, understanding, counsel, and knowledge would rest on the Davidic shoot (Isaiah 11:2). The Spirit of the coming Messiah is now the Spirit through whom believers know the Father — Christ has sent what was promised for himself to be shared with his people.
18: A revelation of God: the prayer for enlightened "eyes of the heart" reverses the condition Paul will describe in Ephesians 4:18 — the darkened understanding of those without Christ. Christ, who is "the light of the world" (John 8:12), has shone in the hearts of believers (2 Corinthians 4:6); Paul's prayer is that this light penetrate further into the full knowledge of hope, inheritance, and power that belong to life in Christ.
19: A direct Christological claim: the "incomparably great power for us who believe" is the same power as the resurrection — it is not a general divine attribute but specifically the power that raised Christ from the dead. Paul's description grounds every assurance of divine power available to believers in the historical event of Easter. The power is not a resource to be accessed apart from Christ; it is bound to his resurrection.
20: A type fulfilled: the resurrection and exaltation of Christ fulfills Psalms 110:1 — "Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool" — the OT's most frequently cited messianic text. The seating at God's right hand is the fulfillment of the royal oracle addressed to the Davidic king. Christ's exaltation is not metaphorical elevation but the literal enthronement that Psalm 110 projected as the destiny of the LORD's anointed.
21: The direct application of Psalms 110:1 continues: Christ is seated "far above all rule and authority, power and dominion, and every name that is invoked." The cosmic powers that dominate the present age are placed beneath the enthroned Christ — not yet visibly subdued ("not only in the present age but also in the one to come"), but already defeated by his resurrection. The "already" and "not yet" of the kingdom are held together in his exaltation.
22: A fulfillment: "And God placed all things under his feet" quotes Psalms 8:6 — the declaration that God crowned the son of man with glory and put everything under his feet. Paul applies it to Christ explicitly (as in 1 Corinthians 15:27): Christ is the Son of Man who receives the universal dominion that Adam was given and forfeited. What the Psalm described as the Adamic mandate is achieved only by the new Adam. The headship over the church is the particular expression of that cosmic lordship.
23: A revelation of God: the church as "the fullness of him who fills everything in every way" names the intimate relationship between the cosmic Christ and his body. The same term plēroma that Paul will use for "the fullness of God" in 3:19 and Colossians 2:9 (where the fullness of deity dwells bodily in Christ) here describes the church as the locus of Christ's fullness in the world. The church is where the all-filling Christ makes himself present and tangible.
